items.q: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
items.correct: Fat (9 kcal/g).
items.why: Students assume carbs (the 'energy food') provide most energy per gram. Carbs and protein = 4 kcal/g; FAT = 9 kcal/g (most energy-dense). Vitamins provide no energy at all.

items.q: Which vitamins are fat-soluble?
items.correct: A, D, E, K.
items.why: Students confuse fat- and water-soluble vitamins. Mnemonic: ADEK fat-soluble (stored in body fat - can build up to toxic levels). B and C water-soluble (excess excreted in urine).

items.q: What is a complete protein?
items.correct: A protein containing all nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts.
items.misconception: A protein with the most calories.
items.random_wrong: Any protein from animal sources only.
items.why: Students think 'complete' means 'high quantity' or 'animal'. It means containing all ESSENTIAL amino acids the body can't make. Most animal proteins are complete; many plants need combining (e.g., rice + beans).

items.q: What is the main function of dietary fibre?
items.correct: Aiding digestion and promoting bowel health.
items.why: Students think all carbohydrates give energy. Fibre is a carb humans can't digest, so it provides NO direct energy. It bulks stools, slows glucose absorption, and feeds gut bacteria.

items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: What temperature range is the food 'danger zone'?
items.correct: 5 to 63 degrees Celsius - where most bacteria multiply rapidly.
items.why: Students assume danger = very hot. It's the MIDDLE range that's dangerous because bacteria thrive there. Fridges stay below 5; cooking exceeds 63 (often 75+ for safety). The middle is where they multiply.

items.q: What does freezing do to bacteria in food?
items.correct: Makes them dormant (slows or halts growth), but does not kill them.
items.misconception: Kills all bacteria in the food.
items.why: Students think freezing sterilises food. It just stops bacteria multiplying - they revive when thawed. That's why thawed food should be cooked promptly and not refrozen raw.

items.q: How does pickling preserve food?
items.correct: Acidic conditions (low pH) prevent most bacteria from growing.
items.misconception: The vinegar kills the food cells.
items.why: Students think pickling poisons bacteria. It works by creating an environment INHOSPITABLE to most microbes - low pH stops them growing, even if they're present.

items.q: Which method of heat transfer cooks food in a microwave?
items.correct: Radiation — microwaves are electromagnetic radiation that makes polar water molecules vibrate, generating heat.
items.misconception: Microwaves cook food from the inside out.
items.why: Microwaves penetrate ~1–2 cm into food and excite water molecules — they cook outer regions first, conducting heat inward. The 'inside out' myth comes from feeling cool outer surfaces after microwaving (because steam carries heat out).

items.q: What is the Maillard reaction?
items.correct: A reaction between amino acids and reducing sugars producing brown colour and complex flavours.
items.why: Students confuse Maillard (protein + sugar) with caramelisation (sugar alone). Maillard gives roast meat its crust, toast its colour, and coffee its richness - different chemistry from caramelisation.

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: Why must the 14 major allergens be highlighted on UK food labels?
items.correct: Reactions can be severe or life-threatening, so consumers must identify them at a glance.
items.misconception: To advertise that the food contains them.
items.why: Students miss the consumer-safety logic. Labelling laws require allergens to be visibly listed (bold/highlighted) so allergic people can quickly check. Penalties for non-compliance can be severe.

items.q: Which vitamin is most likely to be deficient in a poorly planned vegan diet?
items.correct: Vitamin B12.
items.why: Students guess vitamin C (it's the famous one), but plant foods are rich in C. B12 is found almost exclusively in animal products, so vegans need fortified foods or supplements.

items.q: What is the purpose of a star profile?
items.correct: Comparing multiple sensory attributes of products visually on a radar diagram.
items.misconception: Awarding overall scores out of 5 stars.
items.why: Students confuse star profiles with rating systems. Star profiles plot attributes (sweetness, crunch, saltiness, etc.) on radial axes - the SHAPE shows the product's sensory fingerprint, not its quality rating.

items.q: Why does food provenance matter to consumers?
items.correct: It affects environmental impact, quality, ethics, and supports local economies.
items.random_wrong: It tells you the calorie content.
items.why: Students reduce food choice to price. Provenance (origin) raises questions of food miles, fair trade, animal welfare, seasonal availability, and supporting local producers - all consumer concerns.

items.q: What is coagulation of eggs?
items.correct: Proteins denaturing and bonding to form a solid structure when heated.
items.misconception: The egg shell breaking when boiled.
items.why: Students think coagulation is something physical happening to the shell. It's PROTEIN CHEMISTRY: heat unfolds the proteins, which then bond together, turning liquid egg into solid - what makes a fried egg set.

items.q: Why is bread kneaded?
items.correct: To develop gluten - the protein network that gives dough elasticity and traps gas.
items.why: Students think kneading is just mixing. Mixing happens earlier. Kneading specifically develops gluten strands, aligning them to create the stretchy network that holds CO2 bubbles during rising.

items.q: What gas does yeast produce that makes bread rise?
items.correct: Carbon dioxide.
items.why: Students confuse yeast with photosynthesis. Yeast ferments sugar to ETHANOL and CO2 (anaerobic respiration). The CO2 gets trapped by gluten, expanding the dough. Ethanol bakes off.

items.q: What is basal metabolic rate (BMR)?
items.correct: Energy used to maintain basic bodily functions at complete rest.
items.why: Students think BMR is about exercise. It's the baseline energy needed just to STAY ALIVE - breathing, heart, brain, organ function - typically 60-70% of total daily energy use even before any activity.

items.q: What is the difference between glucose and sucrose?
items.correct: Glucose is a monosaccharide (single sugar); sucrose is a disaccharide (glucose + fructose).
items.random_wrong: Glucose is artificial, sucrose is natural.
items.why: Students treat all sugars as identical. Sucrose (table sugar) is two sugars bonded together - the body splits it into glucose and fructose during digestion. Different sugars have different metabolic paths.

items.q: Why are dried foods less prone to spoilage?
items.correct: Low water activity prevents microorganisms from growing.
items.misconception: Bacteria do not like dry textures.
items.why: Students think it's about texture preference. Microbes need available water. Drying reduces WATER ACTIVITY below the threshold microbes can use - no water = no metabolism = no spoilage.

items.q: Are local foods always more sustainable than imported ones?
items.correct: Not always - production methods can outweigh transport emissions.
items.misconception: Yes - shorter distance always means lower emissions.
items.random_wrong: No - imported food is always better.
items.why: Students apply 'shorter is better' too simply. A heated UK greenhouse tomato may have higher emissions than a Spanish field tomato trucked north. Lifecycle analysis matters more than miles alone.

items.q: What is the difference between a food allergy and an intolerance?
items.correct: An allergy involves the immune system; intolerance does not.
items.misconception: They are the same with different severity.
items.random_wrong: Allergies are temporary, intolerances are permanent.
items.why: Students treat these as severity points on one spectrum. They have different mechanisms - allergies trigger immune (IgE) responses (potentially fatal anaphylaxis); intolerances are digestive (e.g., lactase deficiency).
